## Introduction

History is full of myths that shape modern politics. Today we examine one that affects borders, wars, and identity claims to this day.

According to historian John Smith in *Colonial Borders*, page 47: "The original treaty drew lines that no one actually surveyed."

## The Colonial Decision

In 1859, Britain and Guatemala signed an agreement establishing boundaries based on rivers that did not match the actual terrain.

The treaty stated, in Article 3: "The boundary shall follow the Sarstoon River to its source." No survey was conducted.

This meant that both sides interpreted the clause differently for the next 160 years.

## Modern Consequences

Today, the ICJ is deciding the case. If you trace this dispute back, it leads directly to that 1859 document.

The myth that borders were "scientifically drawn" continues to fuel conflict. The primary sources tell a different story.
